title: Anger After Mass Shootings: A Practice for Civilians
dek: *After another headline breaks, your chest tightens and your jaw sets. You refresh the news, argue online, then feel hollow. That cycle isn't dysfunction—it's incomplete biology. Here's how to finish what your nervous system started, so anger becomes fuel instead of poison.*
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. Anger after mass shootings isn't a problem to suppress - it's information your body needs you to act on. The somatic tension you carry can become chronic unless you give it a specific, contained outlet. This practice teaches civilians how to metabolize rage without harming yourself or bystanders.

The counterintuitive truth: your anger after a mass shooting isn't too much. It's too little - or more precisely, it's arrested halfway through its natural arc. You feel the surge, the heat, the tightness, but modern civilian life offers no sanctioned outlet, no way to complete the biological sequence that anger initiates. So it circles back, lands in your shoulders, your gut, your throat, and ferments there. In my own practice, I've watched people carry this unmetabolized rage for years, mistaking its weight for moral seriousness when it's actually just unfinished nervous system business. We've been taught that anger is dangerous, that it leads to violence, that good people transmute it into something gentler. But anger isn't the problem. Stuck anger is. And after mass violence, when the target is diffuse and the threat is systemic, most of us have no idea how to let the energy move through and out. This isn't about catharsis for its own sake. It's about recognizing that your body prepared to fight or flee, flooded your bloodstream with stress hormones, tightened your muscles for action - and then you sat still and scrolled. That gap between arousal and resolution is where the damage happens. ## Why Civilian Anger After Mass Violence Gets Trapped You weren't there. You didn't run, didn't shield anyone, didn't fight back. You heard about it secondhand, maybe saw footage, maybe just read the headline. But your nervous system doesn't parse secondhand threats as less real - it reads danger, activates ancient circuitry, and waits for you to do something. Peter Levine's work on trauma and the body teaches us that mammals shake off stress after a threat passes. A gazelle that escapes a lion will literally tremble, discharging the activation. Humans, especially civilians far from the event, rarely allow that discharge. We refresh the news. We argue. We donate, sign petitions, post statements. All useful, none of it somatic. The anger gets layered with helplessness. You can't undo what happened. You can't personally disarm the shooter or resurrect the dead. The rage has nowhere to land, so it turns inward or sideways - snapping at your partner, grinding your teeth at night, a low-grade irritability that colors everything for weeks. Across the articles we've published on this site, we return again and again to this theme: emotion that doesn't move becomes pathology. Anger is motion. The word itself shares a root with "angst" and "anxiety," all pointing to constriction, narrowing, a tightening that demands release. ## The Physiology of Unmetabolized Rage When you hear about a mass shooting, your body doesn't ask if you're in immediate danger. It reads the signal - violence, threat, chaos - and responds. Your adrenal glands release cortisol and adrenaline. Your heart rate spikes. Blood moves away from your digestive system toward your limbs. Your pupils dilate. You are, for a moment, ready to fight. But you don't fight. You sit at your desk, or your kitchen table, or in your car. The activation has nowhere to go. Over hours and days, if you repeat this cycle - more news, more outrage, more sitting still - you train your body to stay in a state of partial mobilization. Your baseline cortisol creeps up. Your sleep suffers. You might notice tension headaches, jaw pain, digestive trouble, a sense of being wired and tired at once. The CDC's guidance on stress and the body the CDC's guidance on stress and the body confirms what somatic practitioners have known for decades: chronic activation without resolution damages nearly every system. Your immune function drops. Inflammation rises. The body, brilliant and literal, keeps preparing you for a fight that never comes. Some people turn to supplements to ease the nervous system's grip. [Premier Research Labs L-Theanine Double Strength - Pure L Theanine Supplement 200mg](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0DZ42848R?tag=spankyspinola-20) can take the edge off acute stress without sedation, though it won't address the underlying need for discharge. Others find that liver support helps process the metabolic byproducts of prolonged stress - [Planetary Herbals Bupleurum Liver Cleanse (alt SKU)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0002YFGBM?tag=spankyspinola-20) has a long tradition in Chinese medicine for moving stagnant anger out of the organ systems. But no herb or supplement completes the cycle your body started. You have to move the energy yourself. ## What Anger Wants: Direction, Not Suppression Harriet Lerner, in her decades of clinical work, has shown that anger is often a messenger pointing toward a boundary violation or a threat to something you value. After a mass shooting, that message is clear: something precious - safety, innocence, the social contract - has been shattered. Your anger is appropriate. It's proportional. The question isn't whether you should feel it, but what you do with it once it arrives. Suppression doesn't work. You can't think your way out of a physiological state. You can't reason with your amygdala or negotiate with your adrenal glands. Trying to "rise above" the anger or shame yourself for feeling it only adds a second layer of distress. Expression without containment doesn't work either. Venting to friends, raging on social media, punching a pillow - these can feel good in the moment, but research suggests they often rehearse the anger rather than resolve it. You end up more activated, not less. What works is giving the anger a task. A specific, bounded, physical action that allows the energy to complete its arc and discharge. This isn't about revenge or even justice in the legal sense. It's about letting your body finish what it started. ## A Five-Minute Practice for Civilians You'll need a private space, a timer, and something to push against - a wall, a heavy door, a sturdy table. Set the timer for three minutes. Stand facing the wall or object, arms extended, palms flat against the surface. Begin to push. Not casually - push as if you're trying to move the wall. Engage your legs, your core, your back. Let your breath get loud. Let your face contort. Push harder. As you push, let sound come if it wants to. A grunt, a growl, a shout. This isn't performance. No one is watching. You're not trying to be dignified or controlled. You're trying to let the activation move through your arms and out. If tears come, let them. If your jaw wants to clench or your shoulders want to hunch, allow it. You're giving your body permission to do what it's been trying to do since you first heard the news. When the timer goes off, stop pushing. Step back. Shake out your arms, your legs. Bend forward and let your head hang. Take three deep breaths, longer exhale than inhale. Notice what's different. Your heart rate, your jaw, the quality of your thoughts. This practice doesn't fix the problem of mass violence. It doesn't change policy or resurrect the dead. But it completes a cycle in your own nervous system, and that matters. You can't organize, advocate, or grieve effectively when you're carrying unmetabolized rage in your tissues. ## The Trap of Productive Anger vs. Destructive Anger Soraya Chemaly's writing on women's anger (though it applies to anyone taught to suppress rage) makes the case that we've been sold a false binary: anger is either "productive" - channeled into activism, art, change - or "destructive," something to be ashamed of and controlled. But this framing misses the somatic piece. You can do all the productive things - call your representatives, donate, show up to vigils - and still be carrying the physiological charge. The activism doesn't discharge the nervous system activation. They're separate tracks. In our experience, the people who sustain long-term engagement with difficult issues are the ones who've learned to metabolize their rage regularly, in small doses. They don't wait for a breaking point. They don't let the charge build until it explodes sideways. They treat anger like any other form of energy that needs to move through the body - sweat, tears, breath, sound. This doesn't mean you become less angry about injustice. It means the anger stays clean, available, responsive. It doesn't curdle into bitterness or collapse into despair. ## Sensory Anchors: What Completed Anger Feels Like The first time you successfully discharge anger, you might not recognize it. People expect fireworks, catharsis, a dramatic shift. What you often get is quieter: a softening in your chest, a sense of your breath reaching deeper into your belly, a sudden awareness that your jaw isn't clenched anymore. Your hands might tingle slightly, a sign that circulation is returning to your periphery after the stress response pulled blood inward. You might yawn - a parasympathetic signal that your nervous system is downshifting. Colors might look a little brighter, sounds a little sharper, as if a filter's been removed. Some people report a wave of fatigue, the kind that feels clean rather than depleted. Your body's been holding a contracted state, and now it can finally rest. Let yourself rest. Lie down for ten minutes. Drink water. Don't immediately pick up your phone. Over the years I've seen people weep after this practice, not from sadness but from relief. The body knows when it's been heard. ## The Ethical Dimension: Why This Isn't Selfish You might resist this work, feeling it's self-indulgent to tend to your own nervous system when people have died, when families are shattered, when the political situation demands action. But you can't pour from an empty cup, and you can't organize from a dysregulated nervous system. The most effective advocates and activists are the ones who've learned to process their rage without letting it consume them. They show up again and again because they know how to metabolize the grief and fury that would otherwise burn them out. This practice isn't instead of action. It's in service of action. It's how you stay in the fight for years instead of flaming out in months. There's also a relational dimension. When you're carrying unmetabolized anger, you leak it. You snap at your kids, withdraw from your partner, bristle at coworkers. The people around you absorb the charge you haven't discharged. By tending to your own nervous system, you protect the people you love from becoming collateral damage to your unprocessed rage. If sleep is severely disrupted and your body won't downshift even after physical discharge, [NOW L-Tryptophan 1000 mg (60 tabs)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B002JNIAQW?tag=spankyspinola-20) can support serotonin production and help restore a baseline sense of safety. But again, this is support, not substitute. ## Beyond the Individual: Collective Practices Ideally, we'd have cultural containers for collective grief and rage after mass violence. Rituals, gatherings, spaces where people could discharge together under skilled facilitation. Some communities are building this. Somatic activists, trauma-informed organizers, and body-centered therapists are creating group practices that allow for shared catharsis and mutual regulation. If you can find such a space - a rage ritual, a grief circle, a somatic activism workshop - go. The nervous system calms more quickly in the presence of other regulated nervous systems. You'll discharge faster and more completely in community than alone. But in the absence of those containers, you can still do this work solo. You can still give your body what it needs. And as you practice, you become a more regulated presence for others, which is its own form of service. For more structured support in this territory, you might explore [the assessments hub](/assessments), where we track somatic patterns and help you identify your particular flavor of stuck anger. Or visit [our herbal cabinet](/herbs) for plant allies that support the nervous system through prolonged stress. ## Frequently Asked Questions ### Isn't pushing against a wall just symbolic? How does that actually help? The wall isn't symbolic - it's resistance, and your nervous system needs resistance to complete the fight response. When you push hard against an immovable object, you're recruiting the same muscle groups and neural pathways that would activate in a real physical confrontation. Your body doesn't distinguish between "symbolic" and "real" resistance when you're genuinely engaged. The discharge happens because you're allowing the mobilization energy to move through and out, rather than circling back into your tissues. The wall is simply a safe, contained way to let that happen. ### What if I do this practice and don't feel any different? First, check that you're actually pushing hard - not performing effort, but genuinely recruiting your full strength. Many people, especially those who've spent years suppressing anger, hold back even when they think they're going all out. Second, one round might not be enough. If you're carrying weeks or months of accumulated charge, you may need to repeat this practice daily for a while. Third, some people discharge through trembling or tears rather than exertion, so notice what else your body wants to do after you push. Not everyone's anger moves through force - some people need to shake, rock, or vocalize instead. ### Can this practice make me more aggressive or violent? No. Completing the anger cycle actually reduces aggression because you're no longer carrying the unresolved charge that leaks out sideways. The people most at risk for explosive anger are those who never discharge, who keep compressing the activation until it erupts. This practice gives you a controlled outlet, which paradoxically makes you calmer and more measured in your daily interactions. You're less reactive, less volatile, because you're not walking around with a nervous system that's already at 7 out of 10 before anything even happens. ## The Long Practice: Staying Awake Without Drowning This article has given you a short practice, a way to metabolize anger in the immediate aftermath of hearing terrible news. But the longer practice is learning to stay engaged with a violent world without letting that violence colonize your nervous system. That means building a rhythm. After each mass shooting, each headline, each fresh horror, you pause and ask: what does my body need right now? Sometimes it's the wall practice. Sometimes it's a hard run, a long cry, twenty minutes of shaking to loud music. Sometimes it's turning off the news and lying on the ground, letting your weight be held by something solid. You're not trying to become numb. You're trying to become porous - letting the grief and rage move through instead of getting dammed up inside. The people who do this work over years don't become callous or detached. They become more tender, more responsive, more capable of sustained attention to what's broken and what needs building. The mass shootings will keep happening until we change the conditions that allow them. That change will take years, maybe generations, and will require people who can stay in the fight without burning out. Your anger is part of that fight. But only if you know how to carry it without letting it carry you. Only if you metabolize it, again and again, so it stays clean and available instead of turning toxic in your tissues. The practice isn't the solution. But it's how you stay awake and functional while we build toward solutions. And that's no small thing. For [more articles in this journal](/articles) that explore the intersection of somatics, emotion, and daily life, or to continue this inquiry at [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), you're always welcome. The work continues, with or without headlines. Your body knows that. Now you do too. ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated May 28, 2026. In our work on anger after mass shootings: a practice for civilians, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
